title: Tree terminology and representation

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Tree terminology and representation; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Tree terminology and representation
sources.claim: A tree connects nodes by parent-child edges with one root and no cycles, giving depth, height, subtree, and leaf relationships.

sources.claim: A tree connects nodes by parent-child edges with one root and no cycles, giving depth, height, subtree, and leaf relationships.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A tree connects nodes by parent-child edges with one root and no cycles, giving depth, height, subtree, and leaf relationships.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A tree with n nodes has n-1 edges when connected and acyclic. Your goal is to represent and annotate a tree and compute node depths and tree height. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Recursive problem design; Linked lists; Singly linked representation. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: A tree connects nodes by parent-child edges with one root and no cycles, giving depth, height, subtree, and leaf relationships. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: In C, tree nodes and heap buffers need explicit lifetime and bounds; C++ can combine RAII nodes with vector heaps; Java uses object references and PriorityQueue contracts; Python uses objects plus heapq's min-heap functions, while the conceptual invariant stays unchanged.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Empty-tree conventions, n-ary children, stored parents, and representation differ across implementations. The draft therefore rejects: Every connected graph is a tree because all nodes are reachable.

includes: Use root, parent, child, leaf, depth, height and subtree accurately for binary and n-ary trees.
Represent and annotate a tree and compute node depths and tree height.

In C, tree nodes and heap buffers need explicit lifetime and bounds; C++ can combine RAII nodes with vector heaps; Java uses object references and PriorityQueue contracts; Python uses objects plus heapq's min-heap functions, while the conceptual invariant stays unchanged.
excludes: Empty-tree conventions, n-ary children, stored parents, and representation differ across implementations.

outcomes: Represent and annotate a tree and compute node depths and tree height.

examples.title: Tree terminology and representation: language-neutral trace
examples.situation: A tree with n nodes has n-1 edges when connected and acyclic.
walkthrough: Step 1 — State the abstract input and invariant: A tree with n nodes has n-1 edges when connected and acyclic.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: A tree is connected and acyclic; adding one cycle or removing one connecting edge breaks that definition.
examples.result: The conceptual result follows because A tree is connected and acyclic; adding one cycle or removing one connecting edge breaks that definition.

examples.title: Tree terminology and representation: implementation transfer
examples.situation: In C, tree nodes and heap buffers need explicit lifetime and bounds; C++ can combine RAII nodes with vector heaps; Java uses object references and PriorityQueue contracts; Python uses objects plus heapq's min-heap functions, while the conceptual invariant stays unchanged.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Keep the same specification and invariant across all four languages.

Step 2 — Map storage, library, equality, bounds, ownership, and error rules using: In C, tree nodes and heap buffers need explicit lifetime and bounds; C++ can combine RAII nodes with vector heaps; Java uses object references and PriorityQueue contracts; Python uses objects plus heapq's min-heap functions, while the conceptual invariant stays unchanged.
Step 3 — Retest this boundary in the selected implementation: Empty-tree conventions, n-ary children, stored parents, and representation differ across implementations.
examples.result: Concept mastery transfers, while implementation evidence remains language-specific.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Every connected graph is a tree because all nodes are reachable.
misconceptions.correction: A tree is connected and acyclic; adding one cycle or removing one connecting edge breaks that definition.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use an invariant or counterexample to explain why this DSA belief fails: Every connected graph is a tree because all nodes are reachable.

analogy.example: A carefully labeled physical arrangement can introduce Tree terminology and representation, while the canonical invariant and operations remain the actual algorithmic model.
usefulFor: Introducing structure, order, connectivity, or state transition before formal notation
limitations: A physical arrangement does not reproduce asymptotic cost, aliasing, numeric limits, recursion depth, or the four languages' implementation rules.
